description: Don't create a factory when there's only one implementation that won't change. Over-engineering with patterns is as bad as under-engineering without them.
goodCode:
// Just use it directly
Logger logger = new ConsoleLogger();
fix: Ask: Do I have 3+ types? Is the type chosen at runtime? Is creation logic duplicated? If NO to all, skip the factory.

trap: God Factory -- knows everyone's private details
description: A factory that loads SMTP config for email, API keys for SMS, and device tokens for push knows too much. It violates SRP by owning private knowledge of every product type.
fix: Either let products self-configure (Option A) or use Factory Method so each subclass owns its private creation logic (Option B).

trap: String keys without validation -- runtime typo bombs
description: Using raw strings as factory keys means typos compile fine but crash at runtime. 'emal' compiles, but factory.create('emal') throws at runtime.
badCode: Notification n = factory.create("emal"); // typo! compiles fine, crashes at runtime
fix: Use enums for factory keys. Parse strings to enums at system boundaries (API, config). Strings never travel deep into code.

trap: Caching mutable products -- thread safety nightmare
description: Returning the same mutable object from a factory creates shared state. Thread 1 sets CC, Thread 2 sends -- OTP goes to the boss.
fix: New instance for stateful products. Cache only stateless products. Use Supplier/lambda in registry for lazy creation.

trap: Factory Method when there's no shared orchestration
description: If the abstract class only has createProduct() and nothing else, the subclass hierarchy is pointless. Simple Factory would be cleaner.
fix: Factory Method is justified ONLY when the abstract class has shared orchestration (log, retry, audit) around the created product. No shared logic = use Simple Factory.
